terms.etiology_clue: Activation of the oncogenic transcription factor gene MYB. MYB drives proliferation of ACC cells and regulates genes involved in cell cycle control.
Unknown; thought to arise from minor salivary gland rests ○ benign tumor arising from distal portions of parotidductal system, including intercalated ducts & acini.
terms.management_clue: Complete surgical resection of encapsulated mass within adequate margin of surrounding parotid gland tissue toavoid cellular spillage & seeding.Recurrent tumor difficult to treat.Radiation treatment of uncertain effectiveness

terms.etiology_clue: Smoking induced, benign tumor arising from salivary lymphoid tissue in intraparotid & periparotid nodes. Theorized heterotopic salivary gland parenchyma present in preexisting intra- or periparotid lymph nodes.Reported association with Epstein-Barr virus. Patients with multifocal or bilateral lesions
terms.management_clue: Biopsy (or excision) needed for confirmation of benign nature + Resection of mass within collar of normal parotid tissue without injury to intraparotid facial nerve is treatment goal

Low-grade MECa : Wide local excision with preservation of facial nerve, Superficial parotidectomy if possible, Total parotidectomy may be necessary if tumor involves deep lobe Postoperative radiotherapy High-grade MECa: Wide local excision; extended total parotidectomy,Facial nerve sacrifice often necessary, Neck dissection routine, Postoperative radiotherapy with large port

terms.etiology_clue: Likely represents malignancy from pluripotent cell that differentiates in multiple directions while maintainingmalignant potential May represent collision tumor of multiple carcinomas expleomorphic adenoma Carcinoma component of 'true MMT' usually salivary gland carcinoma Sarcoma component usually chondrosarcoma Glandular & spindle cell components common

Lymphangitic or hematogenous spread of tumor Skin of face, external ear, & scalp accounts for 75% of primary tumors Systemic metastases to parotid nodes rare Parotid has intraglandular lymph nodes (unlike submandibular & sublingual glands) Normal parotid: ~ 20 intraglandular nodes Embryology/anatomy Parotid undergoes late encapsulation, incorporating nodes within its parenchyma Parotid
terms.management_clue: Parotidectomy, neck dissection + radiation therapy SCCa: Parotidectomy & neck dissection dictated by imaging & physical exam Postoperative radiotherapy Melanoma: Parotidectomy & neck dissection dictated by lymphatic mapping & sentinel node identification Adjuvant radiotherapy ± chemotherapy depends on context

terms.etiology_clue: it is considered a low-grade malignancy arising from serous acinar cells within salivary glands, most commonly the parotid. Key factors include genetic rearrangements involving the NR4A3 gene, prior radiation exposure, and familial predisposition. It affects a younger population than most salivary tumors, with a slight female preference.
terms.management_clue: complete surgical resection. Parotidectomy (removal of the parotid gland) is the standard approach, often with adjuvant radiation for high-grade or incomplete resection. While often curable, the risk of recurrence requires long-term monitoring

terms.etiology_clue: associated with long-term occupational exposure to inhaled carcinogens, with hardwood dust being the leading cause (especially in furniture manufacturing). Other major etiological factors include exposure to softwood dust, leather dust, nickel, chromium, and textile dust. These agents commonly induce intestinal-type adenocarcinoma (ITAC)
terms.management_clue: management primarily involves surgical resection, often via endoscopic techniques, followed by adjuvant radiation therapy, especially for high-grade tumors or positive margins. Multidisciplinary care is essential, with treatment often combining endoscopic or open surgery (like maxillectomy) and radiotherapy to improve local control and preserve function

terms.etiology_clue: While often arising de novo, about 25% develop from pre-existing pleomorphic adenoma. Key etiologic drivers include HER2 amplification (20–30%) and androgen receptor (AR) overexpression (>75%)
terms.management_clue: Main treatment is wide surgical resection with neck dissection and adjuvant radiation, often with HER2 or AR targeted therapies

terms.etiology_clue: Occupational exposure to leather dust, wood dust, nickel, arsenic, formaldehyde have been suggested ○ Smoking increases risk 2-3xInverted papilloma has 5% conversion rate to SNSCCa (2-29% SNSCCa arise from inverted papilloma)
terms.management_clue: Surgery, followed by radiotherapy in advanced stages Definitive radiotherapy itself may be as good as surgery and postoperative radiotherapy

terms.etiology_clue: Unknown; possibly multifactorial, Environmental, genetic, viral, prior radiation, Increased incidence with autoimmune disorders, Sjögren syndrome most commonly Rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus Increased incidence with immunosuppression
terms.management_clue: Tumor debulking of parotid mass may be performed for cosmetic purposes .Chemotherapy & radiation remain mainstay of treatment

terms.etiology_clue: Arises from differentiated neoplastic Schwann cells wrapping around CNVI
terms.management_clue: Controversial: Tumor is easily dissected from nerve, but facial nerve palsy may result nonetheless Types C and D lesions at greater risk of postoperative facial nerve palsy Choice of treatment depends on function of facial nerve and location of tumor Stereotactic radiosurgery may be used if CNVII intact

terms.etiology_clue: The cause of hemangioma is currently unknown, but several studies have suggested the importance of estrogen signaling in proliferation. Localized soft-tissue hypoxia coupled with increased circulating estrogen after birth may be the stimulus
terms.management_clue: Infantile hemangiomas are common, often harmless, and usually shrink on their own, often requiring only

monitoring. For rapidly growing, large, or ulcerated hemangiomas, treatments like oral propranolol (a beta-blocker) are highly effective. Other options include topical timolol, steroids, or pulsed dye lasers

terms.etiology_clue: it is considered a rare, benign, slow-growing tumor (0.6%–4.4% of parotid tumors) often linked to genetic factors, particularly chromosome 12 rearrangements. Common predisposing factors include middle age (40–60 years), obesity, and trauma, which may cause fat cell proliferation
terms.management_clue: conservative surgical excision (superficial parotidectomy or simple enucleation) with preservation of the facial nerve
